caveats: a: The occupational evidence is four-digit and includes boat, motorcycle, and other dealers.
a: Owner and family labor at independent dealerships may be underrepresented in wage weights.
a: AI exposure does not imply that physical repair, safety judgments, or customer accountability can be removed.
rho: The IDS survey is North American, vendor-sponsored, and does not disclose a population-weighted U.S. LMM sample in the fetched material.
rho: Planning to use AI is not equivalent to operational implementation or realized labor avoidance.
rho: The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
e: The certified-technician count lacks a complete dealership denominator and includes locations rather than firms.
e: Dealer survey priorities do not prove that every respondent already has material recurring-service revenue.
e: The frozen firm count uses a retail margin bridge and the compensation ratio has mismatched wage and receipt vintages.
s5: The 94 transactions span several dealership verticals and are not an RV-specific numerator.
s5: Transactions, rooftops, and firms are different units.
s5: Pandemic-era earnings volatility and aged inventory can delay or prevent otherwise intended transfers.
q: No source directly measures five-year pass-through of AI savings at RV dealers.
q: Capture differs between customer-pay service, manufacturer warranty, insurance work, F&I, and unit sales.
q: This estimate excludes implementation difficulty and demand changes.
d5: Wholesale shipments are not retail sales and can diverge during inventory corrections.
d5: Strong purchase interest is not a purchase probability and is sensitive to financing and household budgets.
d5: The dealer basket mixes cyclical vehicle sales with more durable installed-base service.
o: The accountable operator may shift from a local dealer to a consolidated regional platform or mobile-service network.
o: Some status communication and owner education can move entirely to software without eliminating the dealer relationship.
o: The estimate applies to eligible service-oriented firms, not pure RV research or private-party transactions.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Households purchase or consign RVs and may return for customer-pay repairs, warranty work, parts, accessories, inspections, winterization, storage, and ownership support; revenue mixes high-ticket transactional unit sales and F&I with recurring labor, parts, and service work.
lens.excluded_activities: RV manufacturers, campgrounds, rental-only operators, independent mobile technicians and repair shops classified elsewhere, pure transactional lots without material repeat service, captive finance units, non-control financing vehicles, shells, and internal fleet-maintenance operations.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market recreational-vehicle dealers that sell new or used RVs and provide repeat external-customer service through repair, warranty work, parts and accessories, inspections, storage, consignment support, or related ownership services.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures four-digit Other Motor Vehicle Dealers (NAICS 441200), combining RV, boat, motorcycle, and other dealers, and does not measure AI task exposure. I map its occupation mix to RV dealer workflows: sales, customer service, bookkeeping, scheduling, marketing, F&I documentation, parts lookup, estimates, and work-order communications have language/data exposure, while technicians, vehicle movement, inspection, demonstrations, and repair are mostly physical. RV-specific technician employment is visible within the proxy, but the mix of other dealer types can bias the estimate in either direction.
caveats: The occupational evidence is four-digit and includes boat, motorcycle, and other dealers.
Owner and family labor at independent dealerships may be underrepresented in wage weights.
AI exposure does not imply that physical repair, safety judgments, or customer accountability can be removed.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 441210 payroll-by-occupation file joined to O*NET tasks and dealership time studies separating already-automated work from manual sales, F&I, warranty, parts, service-advisor, technician, and logistics tasks.
a.rationale: The four-digit mix reports 33.15% of employment in sales, 14.22% in office and administrative support, 8.00% in management, and 29.80% in installation, maintenance, and repair; recreational-vehicle service technicians alone are 6.88%. Wage weighting increases management's influence, but the large hands-on repair and movement base constrains substitution. AI can address portions of sales follow-up, inventory content, estimate preparation, service triage, status messaging, documentation, and analytics, leaving roughly one-third of current wage-weighted tasks exposed in the base case.

d5.bridge: RVIA's latest 2026 forecast covers manufacturer wholesale shipments, not constant-price dealer demand, and projects a median 314,000 units, 8.2% below 2025. RVIA owner research measures household ownership, usage, and stated purchase interest rather than completed dealer transactions or service quantity. I combine the near-term shipment downturn with an 8.1 million-household installed base, increased median annual use, and long-run purchase interest to infer a wide five-year range around flat demand. Shipments can understate dealer service demand when the installed base ages, while stated interest likely overstates realized purchases.
caveats: Wholesale shipments are not retail sales and can diverge during inventory corrections.
Strong purchase interest is not a purchase probability and is sensitive to financing and household budgets.
The dealer basket mixes cyclical vehicle sales with more durable installed-base service.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year ITR/RVIA retail-registration forecast joined to dealer-channel share, used-unit sales, installed RV parc by age, service work orders, warranty claims, parts sales, storage demand, and inflation-adjusted revenue from IDS and Lightspeed.
d5.rationale: New-unit demand is discretionary, rate-sensitive, and currently soft, but dealers also serve an installed base of 8.1 million owning households. Median usage rose to 30 days from 20 in the prior study, supporting repair, parts, accessories, and warranty demand, while 16.9 million households express strong five-year purchase interest. The current basket could therefore recover after the near-term unit decline, but financing costs, household budgets, inventory corrections, and channel consolidation justify material downside.

caveats: The certified-technician count lacks a complete dealership denominator and includes locations rather than firms.
Dealer survey priorities do not prove that every respondent already has material recurring-service revenue.
The frozen firm count uses a retail margin bridge and the compensation ratio has mismatched wage and receipt vintages.
e.change_evidence: A rooftop-to-firm census for the 502 frozen-band firms showing customer-pay and warranty work orders, service bays, certified technicians, parts and service gross profit, recurring-customer share, legal ownership, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: RV dealerships commonly pair unit sales with repair, warranty, parts, accessories, and ownership support. IDS reports customer-pay service as dealers' largest non-unit-sales focus and describes recurring revenue beyond the initial sale. RVIA reports nearly 1,000 dealerships employ at least one certified technician. Eligibility is discounted for transactional or used-only lots without material service, group or captive structures, and firms whose margin-bridged EBITDA band is distorted by inventory-heavy retail receipts.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 441210 ($3,587,241,457) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 441210 ($43,296,838,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0724 (margins.json[441]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Automotive) EBITDA/Sales 7.24%); revenue/employee $717,025 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 441210); class <5: 793 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,507,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $109,170 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 439 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,182,007 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $230,377 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 406 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,891,825 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $571,368 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 436 firms, avg revenue/firm $25,304,005 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,832,010 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 66 firms, avg revenue/firm $113,100,485 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,188,475 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,461,764,231 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $105,831,730 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 502

caveats: The accountable operator may shift from a local dealer to a consolidated regional platform or mobile-service network.
Some status communication and owner education can move entirely to software without eliminating the dealer relationship.
The estimate applies to eligible service-oriented firms, not pure RV research or private-party transactions.
o.change_evidence: Dealer and owner data through 2031 on fully online transaction completion, mobile-service share, remote diagnostics, over-the-air repair displacement, outsourced warranty administration, self-installation, and physical technician hours per unit and work order.
o.rationale: Most surviving quantity still needs an accountable RV dealer or comparable operator because 94% of RV transactions occur in person and RVs require physical demonstrations, trade appraisal, preparation, delivery, title and financing, safety-sensitive repair, warranty administration, parts, and customer handoff. Software can self-serve research, scheduling, status updates, document intake, and some diagnostics, but it does not remove the responsible operating entity or hands-on service network within five years.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year pass-through of AI savings at RV dealers.
Capture differs between customer-pay service, manufacturer warranty, insurance work, F&I, and unit sales.
This estimate excludes implementation difficulty and demand changes.
q.change_evidence: Matched dealership P&Ls and repair-order data before and after comparable automation, separating new and used unit margin, F&I, customer-pay labor, warranty reimbursement, parts, discounts, cycle time, volume, and customer retention by local market.
q.rationale: AI-enabled labor savings in dealership overhead and customer-pay service are not contractually passed through, and scarce technician capacity plus long repair cycles can allow throughput gains to be retained. Warranty reimbursements and insurer-paid work constrain pricing, while unit sales remain high-ticket and competitively shopped. Over five years, local and national dealer competition should share part of the benefit through price, service responsiveness, and customer concessions, leaving retention near two-thirds in the base case.

caveats: The IDS survey is North American, vendor-sponsored, and does not disclose a population-weighted U.S. LMM sample in the fetched material.
Planning to use AI is not equivalent to operational implementation or realized labor avoidance.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. RVDA/IDS longitudinal survey reporting production deployments, workflow coverage, integration depth, human overrides, accuracy, repair-cycle effects, and labor hours avoided at the same dealers through 2031.
rho.rationale: Dealer adoption has begun: an IDS 2025 North American survey reports 36% already using AI and 40% planning to, with uses including ads, parts and service estimates, and CRM follow-up. Five-year implementation can extend to appointment intake, work-order status, parts lookup, recall and specification retrieval, estimate drafting, lead nurturing, and bookkeeping. It remains limited by fragmented OEM and DMS data, long repair cycles, warranty approvals, variable unit configurations, safety-critical work, technician capacity, and poor customer reactions to low-quality chatbots.

s5.bridge: An RV-industry acquisition article reports that Performance Brokerage Services facilitated 94 dealership transactions in 2024, but the broker serves RV, marine, powersports, automotive, and commercial-truck dealers and does not disclose the RV-only count or unique-firm denominator. I use the flow, active RV-focused buyer commentary, and evidence of multi-location consolidation as a directional proxy for a five-year qualifying control-transfer probability. The broad transaction count overstates RV-specific activity, while one broker's disclosed volume omits private, direct, and family transfers.
caveats: The 94 transactions span several dealership verticals and are not an RV-specific numerator.
Transactions, rooftops, and firms are different units.
Pandemic-era earnings volatility and aged inventory can delay or prevent otherwise intended transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year RV-only transaction ledger from Performance Brokerage Services, RVDA, Camping World, Blue Compass, and state dealer-license records, deduplicated to selling firms and tagged for control change, rooftop count, EBITDA, family succession, asset liquidation, and failed processes.
s5.rationale: A developed brokerage process, consolidator interest, and dealer discussions about selling support a material control market. Transfers remain constrained by aged inventory, floorplan financing, real estate, manufacturer relationships, warranty obligations, owner dependence, and highly volatile normalized earnings. The range centers on roughly one in five eligible firms changing control within five years rather than treating every dealership transaction as an RV-firm transfer.

principal_driver: A sizable recurring repair, warranty, parts, and customer-support operation provides repeat demand and AI-addressable coordination work beyond the initial RV sale.
principal_weakness: Hands-on service limits labor substitution, while highly cyclical, financing-sensitive unit sales make five-year demand and normalized dealership earnings unusually uncertain.

sources.supports: Reports 175,690 workers and the occupation mix, including 33.15% sales, 14.22% office and administrative support, 8.00% management, 29.80% installation/maintenance/repair, and 6.88% recreational-vehicle service technicians.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 441200 Other Motor Vehicle Dealers

sources.supports: Reports 2025 dealer survey AI use at 36%, planned use at 40%, and no use at 24%; documents uses in ads, estimates, and CRM, service communication workflows, service-cycle metrics, and dealer focus on customer-pay, insurance, and warranty work.
sources.title: IDS 2026 RV Industry Trends Report

sources.supports: Projects 2026 wholesale shipments between 300,000 and 328,100 units with a 314,000 median, 8.2% below the 342,200 units shipped in 2025, citing household-budget and financing pressure.
sources.title: RV RoadSigns Quarterly Forecast: Summer 2026

sources.supports: Reports 8.1 million RV-owning households, 16.9 million households with strong five-year purchase interest, 30 median annual use days, and 94% of transactions occurring in person.
sources.title: Go RVing 2025 RV Owner Demographic Profile and Industry Insights

sources.supports: Reports $38 billion of RV sales and service economic output and describes downstream strength supported by 8.1 million RV-owning households despite plateauing post-pandemic shipments.
sources.title: New Study Details the RV Industry's $159 Billion Economic Impact on the United States Economy

sources.supports: Reports more than 7,500 certified RV technicians and nearly 1,000 dealerships employing at least one certified technician as of 2025.
sources.title: RV Industry Association Publishes 2025 Annual Report

sources.supports: Reports 94 dealership transactions facilitated by Performance Brokerage Services in 2024 across multiple dealer verticals and details RV-dealer valuation, inventory, and transaction considerations.
sources.title: Experts Share Dealership Acquisitions Tips and Tricks

title: Recreational Vehicle Dealers
